executiveSummary: Inference Cost Router sells software that cuts production AI inference spend without breaking latency or quality SLAs. The initial buyer is a CTO or head of AI platform at teams already running meaningful multi-model or multi-region traffic. The MVP is a routing and policy engine that chooses the cheapest acceptable model and GPU path per request, with audit logs and spend telemetry. The wedge is immediate ROI: lower cost per request, lower overprovisioning, and less manual tuning work for platform teams. The market is crowded with AI gateways, but most competitors emphasize access, observability, or governance rather than closed-loop cost optimization. We can win by proving measurable savings on real traffic, transparent routing decisions, and strong multi-cloud support. The main risks are cloud-vendor bundling, weak trust in benchmark-driven routing, and long integration cycles into production inference stacks.
problem: Production AI teams often pick models, regions, and GPU pools manually, so cost and latency drift as workloads change.
Teams overprovision capacity to protect SLAs because they lack a trustworthy, automated routing layer.
Static dashboards show spend after the fact but do not continuously optimize request-level decisions.
solution: A policy engine that routes each inference request across models, regions, and GPU pools to the cheapest option that still meets SLA constraints.
Closed-loop optimization that updates routing based on live latency, quality proxies, failure rates, and unit cost telemetry.
Transparent policy controls, audit logs, and rollback paths so teams can verify and override routing decisions.
whyWeWin: ROI is easy to test because the product can be measured against baseline cost per 1,000 requests and p95 latency.
Routing data, benchmark history, and workload-specific policies compound into a proprietary optimization layer over time.
A multi-cloud and multi-model control plane is more attractive to buyers than a single-vendor routing feature.
Explainable routing with audit logs addresses a known trust barrier better than black-box optimization.

segments: AI product teams running production inference
MLOps and platform teams operating shared AI infrastructure
Enterprise teams with multi-model, multi-region, or regulated inference workloads
market.buyingProcess: Initial adoption typically starts with a platform or AI engineering team running a 2-6 week proof of value on a bounded workload, followed by security and architecture review, then rollout approval from the CTO or head of AI platform if savings and SLA adherence are proven.

product.mvp: Route requests across a limited set of models and regions using customer-defined latency and quality thresholds, show baseline-versus-optimized spend, and provide policy audit logs, failover, and manual override.
product.sixMonth: Add automated policy recommendations, richer observability, A/B routing experiments, and integrations with the most common model gateways and telemetry stacks used by early customers.
product.twelveMonth: Support GPU-pool-aware routing, reservation and burst policy controls, compliance-aware region rules, and workload-specific quality proxy tuning for multiple inference patterns.
product.twentyFourMonth: Evolve into an inference control plane with forecasting, procurement optimization inputs, deeper workflow automation, and expansion into adjacent governance and reliability controls.
keyBets: Customers will trust proxy metrics and audits enough to let routing decisions affect production traffic.
Savings are large enough to justify a new control-plane vendor despite incumbent gateway options.
Multi-provider support can be built faster than incumbents can bundle equivalent optimization.

gtm.wedge: Start with one expensive production workload where routing can cut cost per request within weeks without violating SLAs.
channels: Founder-led direct sales to AI infrastructure leaders
Cloud marketplace distribution after reference deployments
gtm.funnelTargets: Target 30 qualified design-partner conversations, 10 technical evaluations, 5 paid pilots, and 3 production conversions in the first 9 months.
gtm.pricing: Base platform subscription plus usage-based pricing tied to routed inference volume or verified savings; early pilots can use fixed-fee contracts with explicit success criteria.

revenueStreams: Premium support and enterprise deployment services
businessModel.unitOfValue: Routed inference requests under active optimization
expansionLevers: Expand from one workload to all production inference traffic
Add more providers, regions, and policy modules
Upsell governance, forecasting, and advanced observability

strategyMap.northStarMetric: Monthly customer dollars saved net of product fees
inputMetrics: Percentage of eligible traffic routed by the platform
Weekly active policy adjustments per customer
moatsToBuild: Workload-specific routing data and benchmark corpus
Customer trust through transparent policy audits and rollback history
Deep integrations into inference gateways, telemetry, and cloud environments
killCriteria: Fewer than 3 customers achieve at least 15% cost savings without SLA regression in the first 12 months
Pilot conversion to paid production stays below 30% after 10 pilots
A major cloud vendor wins most target deals with a bundled multi-cloud-equivalent feature set
strategyMap.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  Wedge --> MVP --> Proof --> Expansion

verdict.whyBelieve: The pain is budget-visible, the buyer is identifiable, and the value proposition can be proven quickly on live traffic.
verdict.whyDoubt: AI gateways are crowded, cloud bundling risk is real, and trust in automated routing may slow adoption.
verdict.nextDiligence: Interview 10 platform leaders, inspect 3 real pilot datasets, and test whether savings persist after quality and latency controls are enforced.

firstCustomer.title: Head of AI Platform at a mid-market or enterprise SaaS company
firstCustomer.profile: Runs production LLM or multimodel inference, spends materially on API or GPU-backed workloads, and has enough traffic variance that manual tuning is failing.
firstCustomer.trigger: Inference budget spikes, a new model rollout, or latency regressions force the team to revisit routing and capacity decisions.
firstCustomer.buyer: CTO or head of AI platform
firstCustomer.initialContract: Paid pilot covering one workload for 8-12 weeks with success criteria on savings, latency, and operational effort reduction.

mustBeTrue: At least 70% of pilot customers must allow the router to control a meaningful share of production traffic within 60 days of integration.
Median measured cost reduction must reach at least 15% on routed traffic while p95 latency stays within agreed SLA bounds.
At least 3 of the first 5 paid pilots must convert to annual production contracts within 90 days of pilot completion.
No single cloud or model provider may account for more than 50% of routed traffic across the first 10 customers.
Time to first measurable savings must be 30 days or less for at least 4 of the first 5 deployments.
diligenceQuestions: How large is the measurable savings gap after controlling for prompt caching, batching, and simple fallback rules?
Which workloads are easiest to optimize without introducing quality regressions?
What integration surface is required for a production pilot in a typical enterprise stack?
How defensible is the routing engine if incumbents add price-aware policies?
What evidence will make buyers trust automated routing enough to expand traffic share?

riskHeatmap.risk: Cloud vendors bundle similar routing into existing spend commitments
riskHeatmap.leadingIndicator: Target accounts choose native cloud routing during evaluations
riskHeatmap.mitigation: Focus on multi-cloud optimization, neutrality, and faster support for heterogeneous stacks.

riskHeatmap.risk: Customers do not trust automated routing decisions
riskHeatmap.leadingIndicator: Pilots stay in shadow mode and do not graduate to active traffic control
riskHeatmap.mitigation: Provide transparent policies, replay analysis, approval workflows, and instant rollback.

riskHeatmap.risk: Savings are too small after baseline hygiene improvements
riskHeatmap.leadingIndicator: Early pilots show less than 10% net savings after implementation effort
riskHeatmap.mitigation: Target higher-spend, higher-variance workloads first and package baseline assessment before deployment.

riskHeatmap.risk: Integration cycles are longer than startup sales tolerance
riskHeatmap.leadingIndicator: Median time from kickoff to first routed production request exceeds 45 days
riskHeatmap.mitigation: Build gateway integrations, narrow the first-use case, and offer white-glove onboarding.

experimentRoadmap.hypothesis: Platform leaders will prioritize cost-routing if baseline audits expose double-digit waste on a live workload.
experimentRoadmap.successMetric: 10 of 15 interviews confirm an urgent spend-control problem and 5 agree to share pilot data.

experimentRoadmap.hypothesis: A read-only router can identify cheaper acceptable routes before touching production traffic.
experimentRoadmap.successMetric: Prototype recommends lower-cost routes on at least 20% of eligible requests in 3 design-partner datasets.

experimentRoadmap.experiment: Paid pilot on one production workload
experimentRoadmap.hypothesis: Customers will pay for a scoped deployment if success criteria are tied to verified savings and SLA protection.
experimentRoadmap.successMetric: Close 3 paid pilots with explicit baseline and rollback plans.

experimentRoadmap.hypothesis: Operators will accept semi-automated recommendations before full autonomy.
experimentRoadmap.successMetric: At least 50% of recommended policy changes are accepted by pilot customers.

operations: Maintain a benchmark and replay pipeline using customer-approved traffic samples.
Run weekly savings and SLA reviews with early customers.
Track provider pricing, reliability, and region-level constraints in a normalized catalog.
Establish incident response and rollback procedures before active traffic routing.

team.role: Founding engineer with distributed systems and inference infra background
team.why: The product depends on low-latency routing, telemetry pipelines, and reliable integrations.

team.role: Product lead with enterprise platform empathy
team.why: Customer trust, workflow design, and operator UX will drive conversion and expansion.
Early deployments need hands-on integration help and fast feedback loops.

items: Complete 15 customer interviews and 5 baseline audits
Ship shadow-mode MVP with spend telemetry and audit logs
Convert at least 3 pilots to production contracts
Support multi-provider routing with policy recommendations
Expand into enterprise controls and advanced optimization modules
Establish cloud marketplace and partner channels

risks.risk: Incumbent cloud and gateway vendors compress differentiation
risks.mitigation: Prioritize heterogeneous environments, transparent optimization, and fast customer-specific support.

risks.risk: Quality proxies fail to capture real application quality
risks.mitigation: Use customer-approved evaluation sets, start in shadow mode, and expose policy thresholds.

risks.risk: Enterprise adoption slows due to integration and security review burden
risks.mitigation: Limit required data access, support private deployment options, and document architecture clearly.

ask.useOfFundsSummary: Fund core engineering, 3-5 design-partner deployments, benchmark and telemetry infrastructure, and early go-to-market for founder-led sales.
